risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "940 children aged 6–35 months... were enrolled and randomly allocated to either zinc group (n=468) or control group (n=472). A randomization schedule with permuted blocks of fixed length (10 per block) was employed for randomization." Comment: The study reports a randomization schedule using permuted blocks, which indicates a systematic random allocation process rather than a predictable or non-random rule; this is sufficient for Low risk of bias for sequence generation.

Quote: "A randomization schedule with permuted blocks of fixed length (10 per block) was employed for randomization." Comment: This describes the randomization schedule/sequence structure, not how the upcoming assignments were concealed from the person enrolling participants. The report does not mention central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or another explicit concealment mechanism; therefore allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The main study was a double-masked, randomized controlled zinc-supplementation trial..." and children were "randomly allocated to either zinc group ... or control group." Comment: "Double-masked" indicates participants and study personnel were intended to be blinded to assignment. The comparison was zinc versus control/placebo in a supplementation trial, with no evidence in the report that blinding was broken. Although morbidity outcomes (eg, diarrhoea, illness days, ALRI) could be influenced by knowledge of allocation if unblinded, the article positively states masking, so performance bias is judged low risk.

Quote: "The main study was a double-masked, randomized controlled zinc-supplementation trial..." and "Home-visits daily for supplementation and every fifth day for the assessment of morbidity were made to evaluate preventive effects of zinc on diarrhoea, dysentery, and respiratory disease-related morbidity." and "Plasma zinc was estimated using atomic absorption spectrophotometry... C-reactive protein was also estimated on a Cobas Fara analyzer." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by health workers/project physicians for morbidity and by lab/imaging system for biochemical outcomes. The trial is explicitly described as double-masked, which supports blinding of outcome assessors. Although morbidity outcomes (diarrhoea, dysentery, ALRI, pneumonia) involve some judgement and could be influenced if assessors were unblinded, the reported double-masked design indicates assessors were likely blinded; laboratory outcomes were objective and automated.

Quote: "Briefly, 940 children aged 6–35 months ... were enrolled and randomly allocated to either zinc group (n=468) or control group (n=472). At the first home-visit, after recovery from diarrhoeal episode at enrollment (passage of three or fewer stools for three consecutive days), 609 of these children were followed up for six months (long follow-up), and for the rest, the follow-up was terminated after recovery from diarrhoeal episode (n=331)." Quote: "Blood sampling was done ... at the baseline (n=940) and after 120 days of follow-up (n=472)." Comment: The paper reports numbers randomized and numbers included in later follow-up subgroups, but this appears to reflect a study sub-design/subsampling rather than clearly reported post-randomization attrition on the outcomes of interest. For morbidity outcomes, the article does not give per-arm numbers analysed at 120 days/6 months, losses to follow-up by arm, or reasons for any missing outcome data. Because the reported information does not allow calculation of post-randomization attrition for the analysed outcomes by trial arm, the risk of attrition bias is unclear rather than high.
